Hereditary angioedema (HAE) is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by a deficiency (or dysfunction) of the C1 esterase inhibitor (C1INH) leading to release of inhibition of plasma kallikrein which increases plasma bradykinin level.

Hereditary angioedema (HAE) causes increases bradykinin causing angioedema, dry cough, abdominal pain, ...etc.

Bradykinin is produced from high molecular weight kininogen (HMWK) via plasma kallikrein.
High molecular weight kininogen is a plasma protein (alpha globulin) responsible for activating the intrinsic coagulation pathway as well as producing the inflammatory mediator known as bradykinin (via plasma kallikrein). 

Plasma kallikrein, in addition to high molecular weight kininogen (HMWK), is responsible for activating factor XII into XIIa. 

Kallikrein-Kinin system consists of a kallikrein (plasma kallikrein or tissure kallikrein) producing a kinin (bradykinin in the plasma and kallidin in the tissue).

Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) degrades bradykinin into inactive metabolites.

HAE is diagnosed by history (family history, history of a trigger, prodrome, and episodic attacks), physical exam, and lab results.

If you don't have the medications, don't forget to secure the airway...Remember your ABC's (Airway, breathing, circulation).

**Angioedema: Essential Steps You Need to Take Right Away**

Angioedema is a rapid swelling of the deeper layers of the skin, often triggered by allergies, medications, or stress. Here’s what you should do immediately:

1. **Identify the Cause**: Recognize possible triggers like food, medications (e.g., ACE inhibitors), or insect stings.
2. **Administer Antihistamines**: Over-the-counter antihistamines can help control mild cases.
3. **Seek Emergency Help**: If swelling occurs around the throat or face, or breathing becomes difficult, call emergency services immediately.
4. **Follow Up with an Allergist**: To prevent future episodes, consult an allergist for a thorough evaluation and potential treatment plan.

Prompt action is crucial in preventing life-threatening situations like airway obstruction.

title: Idiopathic Angioedema - Case Study #1: The Inflammation Files with Dr. Kara Wada
description:
Case #1 - Itchy Mystery 

Remember Sarah, the young woman struck by sudden, severe hives and swelling?  Yesterday, you played detective, and now it's time for the big reveal...

The answer is B) Idiopathic Angioedema.  But what exactly does that mean?

1️⃣ Swelling That's More Than Skin-Deep: Angioedema causes swelling beneath the skin, which is why Sarah's face was so dramatically affected.

2️⃣ Not Your Average Allergy: Itchy hives are a classic allergy sign, but angioedema often itches less and the swelling is larger and deeper.

3️⃣ The Mystery of "Idiopathic": This means the cause is unknown, adding to the frustration and making diagnosis tricky.

The Good News:  Even though the cause is mysterious, angioedema is usually treatable!  Sarah responded well to medication and is back to enjoying life.

Did this diagnosis surprise you? It highlights how important it is to look beyond the obvious when it comes to allergic-type reactions.

title: O que é Angioedema ou Urticária gigante?
description:
⚠️ O que é angioedema (ou urticária gigante)?
É um inchaço súbito e profundo que atinge a pele ou as mucosas, muitas vezes no rosto, olhos ou garganta.
Pode vir acompanhado de urticária e coceira — e sim, pode ser uma urgência médica, especialmente se dificultar a respiração.
🔎 As causas variam:
– Reações alérgicas
– Medicamentos
– Estresse
– Fatores genéticos
O tratamento pode envolver anti-histamínicos, corticoides e, em casos graves, até epinefrina.

description:
Sudden lip swelling like this isn’t always cosmetic—it’s called angioedema, and we see it all the time in allergy clinics. Possible causes? Viruses, medications (like blood pressure meds), or even unknown triggers. 🤷🏽‍♂️

Hereditary angioedema (HAE) is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by a deficiency (or dysfunction) of the C1 esterase inhibitor (C1INH) leading to release of inhibition of plasma kallikrein which increases plasma bradykinin level.

Hereditary angioedema (HAE) causes increases bradykinin causing angioedema, dry cough, abdominal pain, ...etc.

Bradykinin is produced from high molecular weight kininogen (HMWK) via plasma kallikrein.
High molecular weight kininogen is a plasma protein (alpha globulin) responsible for activating the intrinsic coagulation pathway as well as producing the inflammatory mediator known as bradykinin (via plasma kallikrein). 

Plasma kallikrein, in addition to high molecular weight kininogen (HMWK), is responsible for activating factor XII into XIIa. 

Kallikrein-Kinin system consists of a kallikrein (plasma kallikrein or tissure kallikrein) producing a kinin (bradykinin in the plasma and kallidin in the tissue).

Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) degrades bradykinin into inactive metabolites.

title: Hereditary Angioedema and Mental Health
description:
Hereditary Angioedema and Mental Health
Hereditary Angioedema (HAE) is a rare and potentially life-threatening genetic condition in which patients can develop HAE ‘attacks’ that include episodes of edema (swelling) in various regions of the body including the hands, feet, face and airway.  Swelling in the airway can lead to death by asphyxiation. 

This chronic condition, especially in the recent past, was associated with a plethora of stressors – physically, financially, and emotionally.  One recent study found anxiety and depression to be high in HAE patients.

Recently, we talked to lead investigator of that study, Timothy Craig, DO, Allergy, Immunology, and Respiratory Medicine at Penn State  Hershey Medical. In this interview, Dr Craig talks about the study and why many of those patients are anxious and depressed.

Reference
Fouche AS, Saunders EF, Craig T. Depression and anxiety in patients with hereditary angioedema. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol. 2014;112(4): 371-375.

title: Testing for Hereditary Angioedema is Easy and Inexpensive
description:
Hereditary angioedema (HAE) is a rare genetic disease characterized by repeated edema attacks that can affect any cutaneous or mucosal surface. The most common areas of swelling are the face, larynx, tongue, extremities, stomach, bowels, and genitals. The attacks can last several days but because it is a very rare condition and the symptoms tend to mimic more common allergic reactions, testing for HAE may not occur to some clinicians.

In this video clip, Timothy Craig, DO of the Penn State Milton S. Hershey Medical Center describes some of the symptoms that may warrant testing for HAE.

The test for HAE can include a simple and cost-effective serum C4 level test.  The C4 concentration is almost always decreased during attacks and is usually low between attacks. A C1-inhibitor functional test may also help in the diagnosis.

Testing is important for HAE since traditional treatments for allergic reactions (e.g., antihistamines, corticosteroids) are ineffective. Currently there are seven FDA approved treatments for HAE. Four are focused on the C1 inhibitor (Bertinert, Cinryze, Heagarda, Ruconest) and three alter the bradykinin/kallikrein system (Firazyr, Kalbitor, Takhzyro).

title: Unraveling Angioedema: The Complexity of HAE and Unknown Variants in Canada_Episode 34
description:
In this episode, Dr. Thomas Buttgereit speaks with Dr. Adil Adatia about the latest findings on hereditary angioedema (HAE) in Canada, focusing on the challenges in diagnosing and treating patients with normal C1 inhibitor levels. They explore the differences between well-known genetic variants, discuss the average diagnostic delay of over 10 years, and examine treatment approaches, including long-term prophylaxis and the role of estrogen as a trigger. Additionally, they highlight the need for standardized guidelines in diagnosing and managing this condition to better treatment response. 

Key Learnings from the Episode:

• The average diagnostic delay for hereditary angioedema with normal C1 inhibitor levels exceeds 10 years in Canada. 

• Patients with normal C1 inhibitor function often experience different triggers, with estrogen and stress being more prominent compared to other angioedema variants.  

• A significant heterogeneous group of patients exists, where genetic mutations have yet to be identified. 

• Many patients require multiple long-term prophylaxis treatments, indicating a more severe disease course. 

• Genetic testing has historically been limited, but recent efforts are increasing its role in diagnosis. 

• The lack of standardized diagnostic criteria across Canada makes it difficult to classify and treat patients uniformly. 

• Insurance coverage for treatments like omalizumab varies by province and county, making access to care inconsistent. 

• There is a need for clear definitions of treatment trials and treatment failures to improve clinical decision-making.

title: Angioedema Explained
description:
Angioedema is a condition characterized by the rapid swelling of the deeper layers of the skin, often occurring in the face, lips, throat, and other areas. This condition is caused by the accumulation of fluid in the deeper layers of the skin, leading to pronounced swelling. Angioedema can be a concerning and potentially life-threatening condition, and understanding its causes, symptoms, and treatment options is crucial for effective management.

Causes of Angioedema:

Angioedema can be classified into two main types: allergic and non-allergic.

Allergic Angioedema:

IgE-Mediated: This type of angioedema is triggered by an immune system response to an allergen. The body releases histamine and other chemicals, leading to swelling.
Non-IgE-Mediated: In some cases, angioedema may occur without involving the typical allergic response mediated by immunoglobulin E (IgE). This can be caused by direct activation of mast cells.
Non-Allergic Angioedema:

Hereditary Angioedema (HAE): This is a rare genetic disorder that leads to the overproduction of bradykinin, a substance that causes blood vessels to leak fluid.
Acquired Angioedema: This type is not inherited but can develop later in life due to various factors, including autoimmune conditions, medications, or infections.
Symptoms of Angioedema:

The primary symptom of angioedema is sudden and pronounced swelling, typically in the following areas:

Face, including the lips and eyes
Hands
Feet
Genitals
Throat
In addition to swelling, individuals with angioedema may experience other symptoms such as redness, warmth, and pain in the affected areas. If the swelling occurs in the throat, it can lead to difficulty breathing, which requires immediate medical attention.

Treatment Options:

Antihistamines: For allergic angioedema, antihistamines are often the first line of defense. They work by blocking the action of histamine, a key player in allergic reactions.

Corticosteroids: In more severe cases, corticosteroids may be prescribed to reduce inflammation and suppress the immune response.

Epinephrine: In cases where angioedema is severe and involves the throat, epinephrine may be administered to quickly alleviate symptoms and improve breathing.

Bradykinin Inhibitors: For hereditary angioedema, medications that target bradykinin, such as icatibant and ecallantide, may be prescribed to prevent and relieve symptoms.

Identifying and Avoiding Triggers: In cases of allergic angioedema, identifying and avoiding the trigger allergen is crucial in preventing future episodes.

Conclusion:

Angioedema is a condition that requires prompt attention, especially when it involves the throat and can lead to respiratory difficulties. Understanding the underlying causes, recognizing the symptoms, and seeking appropriate medical care are essential for managing angioedema effectively. Individuals with a history of angioedema should work closely with healthcare professionals to develop a personalized management plan that addresses their specific needs and triggers.

title: How are urticaria and angioedema treated?
description: The most commonly used treatment for urticaria and angioedema is antihistamines. Antihistamines block the effect of the histamine that is released and reduce the itching and rash in most people. If the urticaria occurs frequently then the antihistamines are best taken regularly as they are much better at preventing attacks than reversing them. Very rarely injections of adrenaline (epinephrine) may be required if there are breathing problems caused by urticaria and  angioedema

title: Medical Stories - Hereditary Angioedema (HAE) Lisa's Story
description:
In San Diego, we meet Lisa, who started suffering from mysterious swelling on her abdomen, limbs, and face when she was 12. Her mother also suffered from the same condition, but her doctors were mystified until a life-threatening medical crisis finally led to the diagnosis of a rare disease called hereditary angioedema, or HAE. Today, Lisa is able to better control her HAE and enjoys a full and active life with her husband and young son. 

This episode features in-depth commentary from renowned expert Marc Riedl, MD, Clinical Director of the US HAEA Center at UC San Diego Health.

title: What Is Angioedema And Is Lisinopril Likely To Cause It? - Cardiology Community

What Is Angioedema And Is Lisinopril Likely To Cause It? In this informative video, we will discuss angioedema, a condition that can lead to sudden swelling beneath the skin, particularly in areas like the face, lips, tongue, and throat. Understanding angioedema is important, especially for those taking medications like lisinopril, which is commonly prescribed for high blood pressure and heart issues. We will explain how angioedema occurs, the various types, and the triggers that can lead to this condition. 

We will also highlight the specific risks associated with lisinopril, including the potential for this medication to cause angioedema even after prolonged use without any prior symptoms. Recognizing the signs of angioedema is vital, as timely medical intervention can prevent serious complications. Furthermore, we will discuss alternative medications for those affected by drug-induced angioedema, ensuring that viewers are well-informed about their options for managing blood pressure and cardiovascular health safely.

title: Episode 974: ACE Inhibitor Angioedema
description:
Contributor: Ricky Dhaliwal, MD

 Educational Pearls:

  Angioedema in anaphylaxis  Histamine and mast cell-mediated pathway Treatment:  First line: epinephrine for vasoconstriction and bronchodilation Second line: H1 and H2 antihistamines such as Benadryl and famotidine     ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema  Different pathway from anaphylaxis  ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema is mediated by bradykinins Therefore, anaphylaxis medications are not beneficial in patients with ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema   Leading cause of drug-induced angioedema in the US Patients most commonly present with swelling of the lips, tongue, or face Treatment:  Airway management: varies depending on the severity and progression of the presentation  If awake nasointubation is required, LMX is a 5% lidocaine water-soluble solution that provides anesthesia to the oropharynx   Medications:  Icatibant is a synthetic bradykinin B2-receptor antagonist that can be used in acute treatment Tranexamic acid (TXA) inhibits the plasmin-dependent formation of bradykinin, but the data on this treatment are mixed and limited Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) is thought to degrade high levels of bradykinin with subsequent resolution of angioedema   Discontinue ACE inhibitor      References

title: Do you give steroids to every angioedema patient?
description:
Not all angioedema is steroid responsive but some are. And some people get steroids no matter what. Histamine mediated angioedema does respond to steroids while bradykinin mediated (eg, ACEi induced) does not. 

This video was taken from the Critical Care Lounge. Watch the full episode now!

title: How can one manage Angioedema?-Dr. Aruna Prasad
description: Angioedema is actually an allergic reaction of the skin. So that particular part of the body could be any part of the body becomes reddish, swollen and painful. It can be sometimes itchy also. We need to identify what you are allergic to. It could be food, drugs, dust etc. Identify what is the triggering factor and avoidance of that factor will cure angioedema, plus antihistamines. The combination of sedatives and non-sedative antihistamines will cure angioedema. If it is really severe then you need to take oral steroids also. I don’t think flax seed oil has any role in angioedema.

title: Remédio  que previne angioedema hereditário
description:
Lanadelumabe, também conhecido pelo nome comercial Takhzyro, é um anticorpo monoclonal humanizado utilizado para a prevenção de ataques de angioedema hereditário (AEH) em pacientes com 12 anos de idade ou mais. 

Aqui estão alguns detalhes importantes sobre o lanadelumabe:

1. **Mecanismo de ação**: Lanadelumabe inibe especificamente a calicreína plasmática, uma enzima que está envolvida na produção de bradicinina. A bradicinina é responsável pelo aumento da permeabilidade vascular, que causa o inchaço característico do AEH.

2. **Administração**: Lanadelumabe é administrado por injeção subcutânea. A dose inicial recomendada é de 300 mg a cada duas semanas. Dependendo da resposta ao tratamento, alguns pacientes podem ser capazes de espaçar as injeções para uma vez por mês.

3. **Eficácia**: Estudos clínicos mostraram que lanadelumabe pode reduzir significativamente a frequência de ataques de AEH. Em muitos casos, os pacientes experimentam uma redução nos ataques e uma melhora na qualidade de vida.

4. **Efeitos colaterais**: Os efeitos colaterais mais comuns incluem reações no local da injeção (como dor, vermelhidão e inchaço), infecções do trato respiratório superior, dor de cabeça, erupção cutânea e fadiga.

5. **Aprovação**: Lanadelumabe foi aprovado pela FDA nos Estados Unidos em 2018 e também foi aprovado por outras agências reguladoras, como a EMA (Agência Europeia de Medicamentos).

6. **Considerações**: Como com qualquer medicamento, é importante que o uso de lanadelumabe seja monitorado por um profissional de saúde. A eficácia e segurança do tratamento devem ser avaliadas regularmente, e os pacientes devem ser orientados sobre como administrar as injeções e gerenciar quaisquer efeitos colaterais.

Se você estiver considerando o tratamento com lanadelumabe para AEH, converse com um médico especializado para obter orientação e suporte personalizados.

title: A Closer Look at Hereditary Angioedema
description:
Dr. Hamad Alhameli and Dr. Inmaculada Cerecedo, Staff Physicians in Allergy & Immunology at the Medical Specialty Institute, explain why early recognition of hereditary angioedema is critical and how advances in treatment are helping patients better manage symptoms. 

It is a rare condition that can cause sudden, severe swelling in different parts of the body and may become life-threatening. Symptoms are often misidentified as allergies, leading to years of delayed diagnosis.

title: Dental Treatment: What Dentists Should Know About Angioedema
description:
Originally Release: May 18th, 2017

For more info or to receive 1 CDE credit go to: 

http://www.catapulteducation.com/courses/angioedema-why-dental-professionals-should-learn-about-this-condition 

COURSE SUMMARY

Angioedema is a condition which results in tissue and mucosal swelling due to various etiologies. It can result in significant morbidity, and when associated with airway obstruction, mortality. In this video, Dr. Bernstein will cover the following areas. 

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

1. Define angioedema and the mechanisms of different angioedema subtypes
2. Discuss the evaluation and diagnosis of angioedema
3. Identify treatment options for patients with angioedema
4. Discuss considerations for dental professionals who may have patients with angioedema

SPEAKER DETAILS

Dr. Jonathan A. Bernstein is Professor of Clinical Medicine in the Department of Internal Medicine, Division of Immunology/Allergy Section at the University of Cincinnati Medical Center and partner of the Bernstein Allergy Group and Clinical Research Center.  

Dr. Bernstein has published 250 peer reviewed articles in addition to numerous book chapters on a variety of relevant topics in Allergy and Clinical Immunology. He is the co-editor of the textbook published in 2014, Clinical Asthma: Theory and Practice. He is the editor-in-chief of the Journal of Asthma and on the editorial board of the Journal of Allergy Clinical Immunology, Annals of Allergy, Asthma and Immunology, Allergy Asthma Proceedings and Journal of Angioedema. He is on the medical advisory board of the HAEA organization, on the board of directors of the AAAAI Foundation and Interasma.

Dr. Bernstein has been voted “Best Doctor” in Cincinnati from 1999 to 2016 and has been ranked by US New and World Report as being in the top 1% of doctors in his specialty.

title: What are the Causes and Symptoms of Angioedema? #shorts

title: Why Does Angioedema Cause Tongue Swelling? - The Pro Dentist
description:
Why Does Angioedema Cause Tongue Swelling? In this informative video, we will discuss the important relationship between angioedema and tongue swelling. Angioedema is a condition that leads to rapid swelling of various tissues, including the tongue, due to fluid accumulation. Understanding how this condition affects the body is essential, especially in dental settings where it can pose risks. We will cover the two primary types of angioedema: histamine-mediated and bradykinin-mediated. Each type has distinct triggers and mechanisms, which can influence how they manifest and how they should be treated.

Additionally, we’ll highlight the significance of recognizing the signs of angioedema in a dental context, as tongue swelling can obstruct the airway and lead to serious complications. We will also touch on the treatment options available for both types of angioedema, including medications that can help manage symptoms effectively.

Whether you are a dental professional, a student, or simply someone interested in learning more about angioedema, this video will provide you with essential information. Don’t forget to subscribe to our channel for more informative content on dental health and related topics.

title: Kathrin's Hereditary Angioedema story
description:
In this touching video, Kathrin, a brave HAE patient, talks about the emotional and physical impact of her condition.

If you haven’t heard of HAE before, it stands for Hereditary Angioedema: a rare disease that affects an estimated 1 in 50 thousand people worldwide. 

The condition causes unpredictable attacks of swelling in various parts of the body, including the abdomen, face, feet, genitals, hands and throat. Due to the variety of places these attacks can manifest, misdiagnosis is often an issue. 

Disease awareness and fast diagnosis are vitally important in improving outcomes for patients with HAE.

You can raise awareness and show support for those affected by SHARING videos on your social media platform, LIKING our content or SUBSCRIBING to our channel. Every action helps no matter how small. We appreciate your engagement.

title: What Is Angioedema And How Is It Related To Tongue Swelling? - The Disease Encyclopedia

We will cover the causes of angioedema, including allergic reactions, genetic factors, and certain medications. Urticária é uma irritação na pele comum desencadeada por muitos fatores, incluindo certos alimentos, medicamentos e estresse.
Os sintomas incluem vergões salientes e que causam coceira, vermelhos ou da cor da pele, localizados na superfície cutânea.
Angioedema é o edema da derme profunda e tecidos subcutâneos. Ele geralmente é uma reação aguda mediada por mastócitos causada pela exposição a alérgenos de fármacos, venenos, alimentos, pólen ou pelos de animais.
De acordo com a causa, a urticária é classificada em:
– induzida: quando um fator é responsável por desencadeá-la, como drogas, alimentos, infecções, estímulos físicos (calor, frio, sol, água, pressão);
– espontânea: quando a doença ocorre sem uma causa identificada, também chamada de urticária idiopática.
A caneta de adrenalina autoinjetável é o medicamento a base da substância responsável por controlar e diminuir as reações alérgicas graves. Seu uso ajuda pacientes com histórico de anafilaxia, diminuindo as reações alérgicas rapidamente e proporcionando tempo hábil para obter ajuda médica necessária.
O omalizumab, vendido sob a marca Xolair, é um medicamento usado para tratar asma, pólipos nasais e urticária

title: Ley Ricarte Soto: Inhibidor C1 Esterasa para Angioedema Hereditario - Ley 20.850
Bradykinin vs Histamine Mediated Angioedema in the ED
description:
Angioedema can fool you 👀 Is it Histamine-mediated or Bradykinin mediated angioedema?  Take a listen to PGY-2 Dr. Hannah Mainor as she explains how to tell the difference.

Histamine vs. Bradykinin look alike but behave VERY differently.
One responds to benadryl, steroids and epi 💉… the other needs either c1 esterase inhibitors, icatibant, ecallantide, TXA or FFP 🧬

When in doubt: treat like anaphylaxis, reassess, and then think bradykinin if no improvement is noted. Early Airway intervention is fundamental!

Resources:
PMID: 36121764 (Angioedema in the Emergency Department: An Evidence-Based Update)

Angioedema is a condition that leads to swelling in various parts of the body, particularly the lips, and is often caused by an increase in capillary permeability. title: Top 3 de las alergias más raras: síndrome hipereosinofílico, mastocitosis y angioedema hereditario
description:
En este video, exploramos tres enfermedades alérgicas consideradas raras o huérfanas: el síndrome hipereosinofílico, la mastocitosis y el angioedema hereditario. Estas condiciones, aunque poco comunes, presentan desafíos únicos tanto en su diagnóstico como en su tratamiento.
🔍 Síndrome Hipereosinofílico: Se caracteriza por niveles elevados de eosinófilos en sangre, lo que puede provocar daños tisulares y afectar órganos como los pulmones, el sistema cardiovascular y el tracto digestivo.
🦠 Mastocitosis: Una proliferación anormal de mastocitos que puede llevar a episodios de anafilaxia recurrente y otros síntomas alérgicos severos.
🌿 Angioedema Hereditario: Una rara condición genética que provoca acumulación de bradicinina, generando hinchazones en diversas partes del cuerpo, como el rostro y las vías respiratorias.
Si sospechas que podrías estar padeciendo alguna de estas enfermedades, es fundamental consultar a un especialista en alergología para un diagnóstico adecuado y personalizado.

O angioedema é um edema (inchaço), que acomete camadas profundas da pele e pode estar associado à urticária. Esse inchaço é deformante e pode durar horas a dias. Os locais do corpo mais afetados são as pálpebras e os lábios, mas pode ocorrer em qualquer parte do corpo.
Os corticoides são substâncias utilizadas para reduzir inflamações ou atividade do sistema imunológico do corpo. Dessa forma, os efeitos do corticoide alteram a ação do hormônio cortisol, influenciando no funcionamento de células da pele, tecido gorduroso ou ossos.

Hereditary angioedema (HAE) is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by a deficiency (or dysfunction) of the C1 esterase inhibitor (C1INH) leading to release of inhibition of plasma kallikrein which increases plasma bradykinin level.

Hereditary angioedema (HAE) causes increases bradykinin causing angioedema, dry cough, abdominal pain, ...etc.

Bradykinin is produced from high molecular weight kininogen (HMWK) via plasma kallikrein.
High molecular weight kininogen is a plasma protein (alpha globulin) responsible for activating the intrinsic coagulation pathway as well as producing the inflammatory mediator known as bradykinin (via plasma kallikrein). 

Plasma kallikrein, in addition to high molecular weight kininogen (HMWK), is responsible for activating factor XII into XIIa. 

Kallikrein-Kinin system consists of a kallikrein (plasma kallikrein or tissure kallikrein) producing a kinin (bradykinin in the plasma and kallidin in the tissue).

Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) degrades bradykinin into inactive metabolites.

HAE is diagnosed by history (family history, history of a trigger, prodrome, and episodic attacks), physical exam, and lab results.

If you don't have the medications, don't forget to secure the airway...Remember your ABC's (Airway, breathing, circulation).

Urticária é uma irritação na pele comum desencadeada por muitos fatores, incluindo certos alimentos, medicamentos e estresse.
Os sintomas incluem vergões salientes e que causam coceira, vermelhos ou da cor da pele, localizados na superfície cutânea.
A urticária costuma desaparecer sem tratamento, mas os medicamentos anti-histamínicos geralmente são úteis para melhorar os sintomas.
O angioedema é o inchaço de áreas de tecido subcutâneo que, por vezes, afeta a face e a garganta. O angioedema pode ser uma reação a um fármaco ou outra substância (fator desencadeante), uma doença hereditária, uma complicação rara do câncer ou uma doença imunológica; no entanto, às vezes, a causa é desconhecida.

In this informative video, we will discuss the relationship between ACE inhibitors and angioedema, a condition that can arise from the use of these common medications. title: What happens in Hives and Angioedema? - Dr. Nischal K
description: Hives and angioedema is nothing but an allergic skin reaction. Its not only seen outside but, it can also involve other internal body parts. Commonly people recognize it is as red itchy lesions which just comes and goes off.  You see for some time and without some time its just gone away without leaving behind any marks. This can be because of two things, one you are reacting to something outside, or you are reacting for something which is internally. So whenever you are reacting internally that is called as autoimmune urticaria, where our immune system is not fighting against something outside, but some protein of its own, the body’s own protein. So that is why it is called as autoimmune. Auto itself  and immune is the immune reaction, the resistance power. When these happens these reactions they go on for a longer duration and it can sometimes cripple an individual. Because of the rashes people do feel very feverish, they can have giddiness, they can have vomiting, their blood pressure can drop down. Not only that, when the swelling involves the nose, the throat, they can have a choking feeling and sometimes it can be very fatal too. Second thing is they could be reacting to outside object. It can be a food allergen sometimes people do react to fish or sea foods, eggs, milk products or wheat. These are food allergens what people react to. Other things are commonly the pollen and this is often seasonal and some people are very prone for that and these individuals are called as atopics. They have a very dry skin. They have a family history of probably a skin allergy, bronchial asthma, that is the wheezing or they have a very itchy eyes or a runny nose or lot of sneezing. These individuals are more prone for this type of a reaction.

title: RuDASA OnBoarding 2025  Angioedema   Dr Cascia Day
description: Dr Cascia Day is one of two registered Physician Allergists in South Africa, and is currently completing her PhD on ACE-inhibitor Angioedema Biomarkers and Genetics in Africa with funding support from the Discovery Foundation, and the SAMRC Bongani Mayosi Research Capacity Development Fund. Cascia completed her undergraduate degree at the University of the Witwatersrand in 2010 with distinction; she then completed her Internship at Tygerberg Hospital, and her Community Service at Khayelitsha District Hospital. Cascia underwent further training as a Specialist Physician at the University of Cape Town (UCT) and graduated in 2020. She then went on to sub-specialise in Allergology and Clinical Immunology, graduating in 2023. Cascia is a member of the steering committee for the International Network of Antibiotic Allergy Nations (iNAAN) with a focus on penicillin allergy de-labelling in Africa, and she has been the Co-Chair of the UCT Department of Medicine Research Symposium Committee since 2023. She is a clinician scientist with experience in both original research and industry trials at the UCT Lung Institute Allergy and Immunology Unit,  with a special interest in drug allergy, infectious disease, angioedema, and urticaria. Cascia is passionate about  allergy education, and improving access to care in Africa.

title: Angioedema Hereditário. Tatuagem e piercing: pode ou não pode?
description:
Quem tem Angioedema Hereditário (AEH), precisa ter muito cuidado com quaisquer procedimentos que possam levar a episódios de inchaço como tatuagens ou colocação de piercings.

O AEH é uma doença rara que causa inchaço na pele, trato gastrointestinal e vias respiratórias. Por isso, é importante que os pacientes com esta condição discutam essas questões com seu médico antes de efetuarem mudanças corporais.

Para saber mais sobre a relação entre AEH e tatuagens e piercings, assista ao vídeo em que respondo à pergunta-título do post.

Esta questão faz parte do livro “Doutor, eu tenho Angioedema Hereditário” da GEBRAEH – Grupo de Estudos Brasileiro de Angioedema Hereditário.

Não arrisque sua saúde e segurança, consulte sempre o seu médico antes de fazer qualquer procedimento.

O angioedema hereditário é uma doença rara, caracterizada por crises de edema que acometem o tecido subcutâneo e mucosas de vários órgãos, manifestando-se principalmente por crises de angioedema e dor abdominal.
Sintomas prodrômicos como fadiga, irritabilidade, fraqueza, náusea e eritema marginatum podem preceder o quadro em mais de 50% dos casos, surgindo horas ou até um dia antes. Nessas situações, há liberação de mediadores vasoativos, como a bradicinina, que aumentam a permeabilidade vascular, levando ao edema.

title: Angioedema Day, conoscenza e prevenzione. Sintomi e diagnosi della malattia
Most Missed Cases: Angioedema
description:
Follow along as our expert subspecialist reviews this case of angioedema as demonstrated on a computed tomography (CT) imaging study.  Angioedema represents allergy-mediated soft-tissue swelling that can lead to life-threatening airway obstruction. Allergic reactions leading to angioedema can be triggered by certain types of foods, medications, and insect bites and stings.

Over the last 10 years, the Department of Radiology at the University of Florida College of Medicine has conducted a simulation-based evaluation of radiology resident competence in critical care imaging.
 
243 residents interpreted this case of angioedema as one of 65 cases during an 8-hour simulated on-call shift, with a median score of 0 out of 10 and an overall average score of 2.70 out of 10.

On average, 4.35 points out of 10 were lost due to missed observations. At the same time, 2.83 points were lost due to interpretive errors.

We define an effective report to be one which achieves scores between 7 and 10. In terms of letter grades, this would be A & B. In this “most missed” case 22% of residents produced effective reports.

We define a report having a critical error to be one with scores between 0 and 2. In terms of letter grades, this would be F & D. In this “most missed” case 65% of residents produced reports with critical error(s).

View the full DICOM set and final report here: https://bit.ly/3qkFwVt

Claim 0.5 AMA PRA Category 1 CME Credit™ per case by reviewing this case and others available in our Decision Support critical care case library. Use WIDIonline to fill in your knowledge gaps by reviewing our educational curriculum on this subject.  

WIDI Online: https://widionline.xray.ufl.edu/

title: ⚠️ A classic case of acute allergic reaction ( Urticaria with Angioedema) #dermatology #skinallergy
description:
The patient developed sudden itching, rashes, facial swelling, and breathing difficulty shortly after eating prawns.

Seafood, including prawns, crabs, lobsters, and certain fish is among the most common food allergens in adults.

This is a Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction, an immediate allergy that can rapidly progress to anaphylaxis, a medical emergency.

These reactions are triggered when the immune system mistakenly identifies specific proteins in seafood as harmful. Even trace amounts can provoke symptoms ranging from skin rashes to life-threatening breathing difficulties.

🚨 In such situations, seek urgent medical care.
Once stabilized, allergy testing and long-term management help identify triggers and prevent recurrence.

title: Invisible Swells: Surviving with Hereditary Angioedema Type III
description:
In this episode of It Happened To Me, we sit down with the multi-talented Sally Pirie, a comic artist, painter, professor, toymaker, and rare disease advocate, to explore her journey living with Hereditary Angioedema Type III (HAE-3). Sally’s path to diagnosis was long, painful, and emotionally fraught, culminating in a deeply moving feature in The New York Times that helped shine a national spotlight on HAE and the broader diagnostic odyssey that so many rare disease patients face.

Sally opens up about the unpredictable flares of HAE, the mental toll of being misunderstood by the medical system, and how she channels her experiences into art, humor, and education. 

She is also Professor of Child and Family Studies and Director and Master Artist at the Comics-Based Research Lab at the University of Massachusetts, Amherst. An award-winning newspaper cartoonist and an anthropologist of childhood and infancy, she received her PhD from the University of Colorado, Boulder and is a graduate of Punahou School and Grinnell College. Her areas of expertise include comics-based research methods, ethnographic research and transgender childhoods. She was the 2020 Distinguished Visiting Professor of Liberal Arts at the University of Minnesota, Morris and the 2021 recipient of the Grinnell College Alumni Award in recognition of her lifetime of public service. She once went to New Year’s Eve fireworks dressed as a huge blue pufferfish because why not. Life should be suffused with art and joy. 

Topics We Cover:
What is Hereditary Angioedema Type III, and how is it different from other forms?
Sally’s early symptoms, misdiagnoses, and emotional health during the diagnostic odyssey
Her daughter’s experience with HAE while taking gender-affirming estrogen and being the only documented transgender person with the condition 
The significance of her New York Times feature 
What it feels like to experience an HAE attack and how deadly it can be
Navigating academia and advocacy while managing a chronic illness
How comics and illustration became a therapeutic and educational tool
Invisible illness misconceptions and systemic barriers in rare disease care
The importance of laughter, joy, and resilience in the face of adversity
Advice for undiagnosed patients and caregivers supporting loved ones with rare conditions
You can learn more about Sally on her website. She also highly recommends the Hereditary Angioedema Association for resources. And be sure to read her feature in the New York Times here. 
 
Stay tuned for the next new episode of “It Happened To Me”! In the meantime, you can listen to our previous episodes on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, streaming on the website, or any other podcast player by searching, “It Happened To Me”. 

“It Happened To Me” is created and hosted by Cathy Gildenhorn and Beth Glassman. DNA Today’s Kira Dineen is our executive producer and marketing lead. Amanda Andreoli is our associate producer. Ashlyn Enokian is our graphic designer.

See what else we are up to on Twitter, Instagram, Facebook, YouTube and our website, ItHappenedToMePod.com. Questions/inquiries can be sent to ItHappenedToMePod@gmail.com.

title: Non-Histamine Mediated Angioedema - (Dr. Mirrakhimov)
Hereditary Angioedema
Angioedema..#shorts #facts #medicalshorts #alvinpsthepa
description:
Angioedema...Continue Reading ⬜⬜➡️

It is a swelling that happens just below the surface of the skin, most often around the lips and eyes. When you have an allergic reaction, the body produces histamine, which causes blood vessels to swell.

⬜ There are two basic types of angioedema:

◾Hereditary angioedema (HAE), a rare genetic condition
◾Acquired angioedema (AAE)

📂 SIGNS & SYMPTOMS:

◾Red welts(scars) that suddenly appear, especially near the eyes and lips, but also on the hands, feet, and the inside of the throat.

◾Burning, painful, swollen, sometimes itchy areas.

◾Discolored patches or rash on the hands, feet, face, or genitals.

◾More rarely, hoarseness, a tight or swollen throat, or trouble breathing.

◾Hives , itching, fever, muscle pain, decreased urine, weight gain, and high white blood cell count occur.

📂 CAUSES:

◾Certain Food
◾Medications like penicillin,aspirin, etc
◾Airborne allergens like pollen
◾Insect bites and infections

📂 Who at Risk ?

◾Having hives or angioedema before
◾Having a body-wide allergic reaction in the past
◾Stress or anxiety

📂 DIAGNOSIS:

•Skin & Allergy Skin Tests

📂 TREATMENT:

If symptoms are mild, you may not need treatment. Hives and angioedema often clear up on their own. But for relief following are given:

◾Anti-itch drugs 
◾Drugs that suppress the immune system
◾Drugs for hereditary angioedema
◾Anti-inflammatory drugs
(🛑 NEVER TAKE SELF MEDICATION)

☑️ Reference: https://www.stlukes-stl.com/health-content/medicine/33/002350.htm

title: Swelling Without A Cause? It could be Hereditary Angioedema!
description:
Hereditary angioedema (HAE) is a rare, potentially life-threatening genetic disorder that causes recurring swelling attacks. 
Unlike allergic angioedema, the swelling in HAE is typically not itchy. 

While attacks can occur spontaneously, triggers like minor trauma, stress, infections, or certain medications (e.g., ACE inhibitors) may precipitate an episode. 

Diagnosis is usually based on clinical presentation, family history, and laboratory testing, including C4 levels, C1-inhibitor protein and functional assays, and genetic testing.

title: Angioedema em Cães! Reação Alérgica? @canilpedradeguaratiba #dicaspet
description:
Angioedema em Cães! Reação Alérgica?
O angioedema em cães é caracterizado por uma reação alérgica na pele e nas mucosas, que pode afetar o seu cão de diversas maneiras. Existem vários tipos de sintomas aos quais pode estar atento.
Em primeiro lugar, o angioedema hereditário ocorre quando existe um défice ou um problema no funcionamento inibidor da esterase C1 (um derivado do plasma). Este angioedema não provoca alergia, mas é genético, mas pode provocar aumento da concentração de algumas moléculas, como a bradicinina, aumentando a permeabilidade dos vasos. 
Em segundo lugar, o angioedema adquirido é provocado por um estímulo exterior, tendo vários tipos. O primeiro deve-se ao consumo excessivo de C1-INH e está associado a síndromes linfoproliferativos (ocorrem em várias condições em que os glóbulos brancos se proliferam em quantidades excessivas) e doenças autoimunes.  Já no segundo, há a presença de autoanticorpos do C1-INH, neutralizando a sua atividade sem afetar os seus níveis. Geralmente está associado a disglobulinemia (anomalia das imunoglobulinas plasmáticas) de origem desconhecida. 
Em terceiro lugar, verificamos o angioedema por hemodiálise, que pode causar choque hipovolémico e em consequência a morte do cão. Por último, um tipo mais raro de se manifestar, é o angioedema por IECA, causado pelo consumo de medicamentos inibidores da enzima conversora da angiotensina (IECA). Estes medicamentos são utilizados no tratamento da insuficiência cardíaca crónica e da hipertensão arterial, para diminuir a resistência vascular sistémica e as tensões arteriais. Não é comum que causem angioedema, mas pode acontecer. @canilpedradeguaratiba #dicaspet #angioedema

Epinephrine in Angioedema  #criticalcare #emergencymedicine
description:
Should we give epi in angioedema?
-Textbook answer is no as angioedema w/ different mechanism to anaphylaxis
-However, some anaphylaxis can have angioedema (more likely if rash present)
-If patient w/ significant airway swelling/compromise, give epi: if anaphylaxis, will rapidly improve patient + if angioedema, won’t harm patient
-If mild swelling, no airway compromise + no other signs of anaphylaxis/allergy, would hold epi

title: What Other Drugs Can Cause Angioedema? - Hypertension Help Hub

What Other Drugs Can Cause Angioedema? In this informative video, we will discuss the relationship between hypertension medications and the potential side effect of angioedema. Angioedema is a condition that causes swelling beneath the skin, particularly around the face, lips, tongue, or throat. While many may associate this reaction with ACE inhibitors, it’s important to know that other medications can also contribute to this condition. 

We will explore various classes of blood pressure medications, including angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) and direct renin inhibitors, and their association with angioedema. Understanding how these medications interact with the body and the mechanisms behind angioedema can help you stay informed about your health.

La urticaria, también denominada sarpullido, es una reacción cutánea que causa ronchas con picazón, cuyo tamaño varía desde manchas pequeñas hasta manchas grandes. La urticaria puede deberse a muchas situaciones y sustancias, incluidos ciertos alimentos y medicamentos.
El angioedema puede surgir con urticaria o solo. Causa hinchazón en las capas más profundas de la piel, a menudo alrededor de la cara y los labios. La urticaria y el angioedema de corta duración (agudos) son comunes. La mayoría de las veces, son inofensivos, desaparecen en un día y no dejan marcas duraderas, incluso sin tratamiento. La urticaria que dura más de seis semanas se llama urticaria crónica.
Canal Medicin.

title: Common Causes of Swollen Lips #shorts
description:
Common Causes of Swollen Lips

Full video at https://youtu.be/LX3ktPeypf4

*Education not medical advice

Allergic Reaction: 
Food: Eggs, milk, peanuts, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, wheat, soy
Medications: Penicillin, lisinopril, Stop the medication and contact your healthcare provider
Material: Cosmetics: Stop using
Insect bites

If you have shortness of breath or tightening of the throat call 911 or your local emergency number.

There is no cure for food allergies but you can prevent allergic reactions by avoiding the things you are allergic to.

If you’re having a mild allergic reaction you can take diphenhydramine: Benadryl. 

Oral Allergy Syndrome
Is caused by minor reactions to some raw fruits and vegetables. A contact allergy only affects the part of the mouth the allergen has touched. 
Angioedema: develops due to an allergy such as an allergy to a common medication called lisinopril
You may also develop a raised itchy rash called hives
Pimples near the lip can cause the lip to swell
Skin infection: Cellulitis
Cold sores: herpes virus
Trauma/Injury: Lip biting, getting hit: Put a cold compress on the lip
Infection of the mouth or gums can cause the lips to swell
Sunburn

RARE:

Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome: that causes swelling in the lips and face, as well as some muscle paralysis.

Treatments:

Stop the offending agent
Apply a cold compress: don’t put ice directly on the lip. This can cause more damage
If it’s a cold sore outbreak use: antiviral cream such as Abreva
For swollen lips caused by inflammation or injury, you can take NSAIDS such as ibuprofen (Advil). Take with food and don’t take if you have uncontrolled high blood pressure or kidney disease (decreased kidney function).

Swollen Lips is also called as Angioedema where people will notice swelling of the lips as soon as they get up in the early morning, as well as it reduces by evening. title: Causes of Facial Swelling | Why Your Face Becomes Puffy | Medical Explanation
description:
Causes of Facial Swelling | Why Your Face Becomes Puffy | Medical Explanation
Hello Everyone I am Dr Shreyashi Chatterjee and I Welcome all of You to my Youtube Channel The Medical Diary of Doctor Chatterjee. Today in this video I am going to talk about Causes of Facial Swelling | Why Your Face Becomes Puffy | Medical Explanation
Facial swelling is a common symptom that can be caused by many different medical conditions. Sometimes it is temporary and harmless, but in other cases, it may signal an underlying health issue that needs urgent attention. In this video, we will explore the main causes of facial swelling in detail, so you can understand why it happens, when to worry, and when to seek medical help.

✅ What you will learn in this video:

Common medical and non-medical causes of facial swelling

How allergies can trigger swelling in the face

The role of infections such as sinusitis, cellulitis, and dental abscesses

Kidney, liver, and thyroid problems leading to puffiness

The effect of medications, trauma, and insect bites

When facial swelling becomes a medical emergency

Main Causes of Facial Swelling Explained:

🔹 Allergic Reactions
One of the most common causes. Swelling can happen due to food allergies, insect bites, or drug reactions. Severe cases, like anaphylaxis, can be life-threatening and need immediate medical care.

🔹 Infections

Sinus infection (sinusitis): Causes swelling around the eyes and cheeks.

Dental abscess: Infection in a tooth or gum can spread to the face.

Skin infection (cellulitis): Red, painful swelling that spreads rapidly.

🔹 Kidney Problems
Kidney disease can cause puffiness around the eyes and face due to fluid retention. This is often seen in the morning and may be linked with swelling in the legs and body.

🔹 Liver Disease
Liver problems like cirrhosis can reduce protein levels in the blood, leading to swelling of the face, abdomen, and legs.

🔹 Thyroid Disorders

Hypothyroidism (low thyroid): Can cause puffiness, especially around the eyes.

Hyperthyroidism (Graves’ disease): May cause bulging eyes and facial swelling.

🔹 Heart Failure
If the heart is not pumping properly, fluid builds up in the body, including the face, ankles, and abdomen.

🔹 Medication Side Effects
Steroids, blood pressure medicines, and some pain relievers can lead to swelling in the face as a side effect.

🔹 Trauma and Injury
Any injury to the face, jaw, or head may cause swelling due to bleeding or inflammation.

🔹 Insect Bites or Stings
Can cause sudden, localized swelling, sometimes severe if allergic.

🔹 Other Causes

Hormonal changes (like pregnancy).

Excessive salt intake leading to fluid retention.

Rare conditions like angioedema (sudden deep swelling under the skin).

When to See a Doctor Immediately:

If facial swelling is sudden and associated with difficulty breathing or swallowing.

If swelling is painful, red, and spreading quickly (may indicate infection).

If you have swelling along with chest pain, shortness of breath, or severe headache.

If swelling persists for weeks without a clear reason.


Facial swelling can range from harmless to life-threatening. Identifying the cause is crucial for proper treatment. Always consult a doctor if the swelling is sudden, severe, or persistent.


This video is for educational purposes only. It does not replace professional medical advice. Always consult your doctor for diagnosis and treatment.

title: Why Is My Tongue Swollen? #shorts #allergies
description:
Is your tongue feeling swollen up? Why would that happen? I'm Dr. Manan Shah, I am an ENT and allergist. Your tongue, like any other tissue in your body, can get swollen. And the most common cause of swelling of your tongue is an allergy. It's usually due to food, something like shellfish or peanuts. And what will happen is your lips or your tongue will get swollen.

If this happens, you have to go see an allergist or if you're having any trouble breathing, you have to go to an emergency room. Basically, when your tongue or your lips swell, if it swells enough, it can swell back in and block off your airway and you can have trouble breathing. If this happens repeatedly, allergy testing can help you identify what's causing it.

And the other cause that I want you guys all to know about is sometimes certain blood pressure medications like lisinopril can cause a thing called angioedema. And the angioedema, again, is swelling of your lips or your tongue. If you'd like to learn more, please subscribe.

title: LIPS/TONGUE SWELLING + CAN’T BREATHE — EMERGENCY? ⚠
description:
Fast swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat plus trouble breathing, hives, or that “something is very wrong” feeling can be a severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis). Doctors treat that like an emergency because swelling can block the airway and blood pressure can crash. This is “get help now,” not “watch and wait.”

title: How QULIPTA Patient Kelsi Is Preventing Her Monthly Migraine Attacks
description:
For QULIPTA patient Kelsi, migraine was more than just a headache: at one point, it defined her. Last-minute cancellations were the norm.

Hear from Kelsi how frequent migraine attacks made her press pause on life--causing her to miss out on college fun and time with her kids.

Listen in to hear how QULIPTA helps Kelsi prevent migraine attacks. While you’ll never truly forget migraine, QULIPTA reduces attacks to help give that forget-you-get migraine feeling. In 3-month studies, QULIPTA helped significantly reduce monthly migraine days. Individual results may vary.

Kelsi is an actual QULIPTA patient who was prescribed therapy when she provided this testimonial and was compensated for sharing her story.
 
Meet more QULIPTA patients: https://www.qulipta.com/patient-stories

What is QULIPTA® (atogepant)?
QULIPTA is a prescription medicine used for the preventive treatment of migraine in adults. 

IMPORTANT SAFETY INFORMATION 
Do not take QULIPTA if you have had an allergic reaction to atogepant or any ingredients in QULIPTA. 

Before taking QULIPTA, tell your healthcare provider about all your medical conditions, including if you: 
• Have high blood pressure 
• Have circulation problems in your fingers and toes 
• Have kidney problems or are on dialysis 
• Have liver problems 
• Are pregnant or plan to become pregnant 
• Are breastfeeding or plan to breastfeed

Tell your healthcare provider about all the medicines you take, including prescription and over-the-counter medicines, vitamins, and herbal supplements. QULIPTA may affect the way other medicines work, and other medicines may affect how QULIPTA works. Your healthcare provider may need to change the dose of QULIPTA when taken with certain other medicines. 

QULIPTA can cause serious side effects, including: 
• Allergic (hypersensitivity) reactions, including anaphylaxis: Serious allergic reactions can happen when you take QULIPTA or days after. Stop taking QULIPTA and get emergency medical help right away if you get any of the following symptoms, which may be part of a serious allergic reaction: swelling of the face, lips, or tongue; itching; trouble breathing; hives; or rash. 
• High blood pressure: New or worsening of high blood pressure can happen. Contact your healthcare provider if you have an increase in blood pressure. 
• Raynaud’s phenomenon: A type of circulation problem can worsen or happen. Raynaud’s phenomenon can lead to your fingers or toes feeling numb, cool, or painful, or changing color from pale, to blue, to red. Contact your healthcare provider if these symptoms occur. 

The most common side effects of QULIPTA are nausea, constipation, and fatigue/sleepiness. 
These are not all the possible side effects of QULIPTA. 

QULIPTA is available in 10 mg, 30 mg, and 60 mg tablets. 

You are encouraged to report negative side effects of prescription drugs to the FDA. Visit www.fda.gov/medwatch or call 1-800-FDA-1088. 

If you are having difficulty paying for your medicine, AbbVie may be able to help. Visit AbbVie.com/PatientAccessSupport to learn more.

Ask your doctor whether QULIPTA is right for you.

title: Clerkship in Pediatrics; Learning to Manage Children Allergies and Anaphylaxis Safely
description:
Managing children with allergies and anaphylaxis safely during your pediatrics clerkship is essential, because these conditions can escalate quickly and require immediate, structured intervention. Here’s a clear guide grounded in current pediatric allergy practice parameters:

🧩 Common Pediatric Allergies
Food allergies: Milk, eggs, peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish.

Drug allergies: Antibiotics (penicillin, sulfa drugs).

Environmental allergies: Dust mites, pollen, animal dander.

Insect stings: Bees, wasps.

⚠️ Recognizing Anaphylaxis
Early recognition is critical. Look for:

Skin/mucosal signs: Hives, swelling of lips/tongue.

Respiratory distress: Wheezing, stridor, difficulty breathing.

Cardiovascular collapse: Dizziness, fainting, hypotension.

Gastrointestinal symptoms: Vomiting, abdominal pain.

🩺 Management Principles
Immediate action

Epinephrine (IM) is the first-line treatment—administer promptly in the thigh.

Call for emergency support and prepare for airway management.

Supportive care

Oxygen, IV fluids, antihistamines, corticosteroids (adjuncts, not substitutes for epinephrine).

Continuous monitoring of vitals.

Post-crisis care

Observe for biphasic reactions (recurrence within hours).

Prescribe epinephrine auto-injectors for future emergencies.

Educate families and schools on recognition and response.

🌟 Clerkship Takeaways
Epinephrine saves lives—never delay its use in suspected anaphylaxis.

Learn to differentiate mild allergic reactions from anaphylaxis.

Always involve families and schools in prevention and emergency planning.

Documentation and clear communication are vital for continuity of care

title: CAUSES OF SWOLLEN LIPS AND HOW TO TREAT
description:
Common Causes of Swollen Lips

Allergic Reaction: 
Food: Eggs, milk, peanuts, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, wheat, soy
Medications: Penicillin, lisinopril, Stop the medication and contact your healthcare provider
Material: Cosmetics: Stop using
Insect bites

If you have shortness of breath or tightening of the throat call 911 or your local emergency number.

There is no cure for food allergies but you can prevent allergic reactions by avoiding the things you are allergic to.

If you’re having a mild allergic reaction you can take diphenhydramine: Benadryl. 

Oral Allergy Syndrome
Is caused by minor reactions to some raw fruits and vegetables. A contact allergy only affects the part of the mouth the allergen has touched. 
Angioedema: develops due to an allergy such as an allergy to a common medication called lisinopril
You may also develop a raised itchy rash called hives
Pimples near the lip can cause the lip to swell
Skin infection: Cellulitis
Cold sores: herpes virus
Trauma/Injury: Lip biting, getting hit: Put a cold compress on the lip
Infection of the mouth or gums can cause the lips to swell
Sunburn

RARE:

Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome: that causes swelling in the lips and face, as well as some muscle paralysis.

Treatments:

Stop the offending agent
Apply a cold compress: don’t put ice directly on the lip. This can cause more damage
If it’s a cold sore outbreak use: antiviral cream such as Abreva
For swollen lips caused by inflammation or injury, you can take NSAIDS such as ibuprofen (Advil). Take with food and don’t take if you have uncontrolled high blood pressure or kidney disease (decreased kidney function).
